docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text: This guide will help you set up a local development environment for App Health Monitor.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Install the following before starting:

  • Python 3.12+ - Download
  • Git - Download
  • pip - Included with Python
  • virtualenv (optional) - pip install virtualenv

Optional but Recommended: - VS Code or PyCharm - IDE with Python support - Docker Desktop - For containerized testing - PostgreSQL - For testing with PostgreSQL

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/mgboyle/App-health-monitor.git
cd App-health-monitor

docs.title: 2. Create Virtual Environment
docs.text:
# Create virtual environment
python3 -m venv venv

# Activate it
source venv/bin/activate  # Linux/macOS
venv\Scripts\activate     # Windows

docs.title: 3. Install Dependencies
docs.text:
# Install all dependencies (including dev tools)
pip install -r requirements-dev.txt

This installs: - Flask, SQLAlchemy (runtime) - pytest, pytest-cov (testing) - mkdocs (documentation)

docs.title: 4. Set Up Environment Variables
docs.text:
# Copy sample config
cp config.sample.env .env

# Edit if needed (optional for development)
nano .env

docs.title: 5. Run the Application
docs.text:
python run.py

Application starts at http://localhost:5000

docs.title: 6. Run Tests
docs.text:
pytest tests/ -v

All tests should pass ✅

docs.title: Git Hooks
docs.text:
Set up pre-commit hooks:

# Install pre-commit
pip install pre-commit

# Create .pre-commit-config.yaml
cat > .pre-commit-config.yaml << 'EOF'
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/psf/black
    rev: 23.7.0
    hooks:
      - id: black
        language_version: python3.12
  - repo: https://github.com/pycqa/flake8
    rev: 6.0.0
    hooks:
      - id: flake8
        args: [--max-line-length=100]
  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v4.4.0
    hooks:
      - id: trailing-whitespace
      - id: end-of-file-fixer
      - id: check-yaml
      - id: check-added-large-files
EOF

# Install hooks
pre-commit install

# Run manually
pre-commit run --all-files

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
App Health Monitor uses pytest as the testing framework with the following test types:

  • Unit Tests - Test individual functions and methods
  • Integration Tests - Test component interactions
  • End-to-End Tests - Test complete workflows

docs.title: Basic Test Execution
docs.text:
# Run all tests
pytest tests/ -v

# Run with coverage
pytest tests/ -v --cov=app --cov-report=term-missing

# Generate HTML coverage report
pytest tests/ -v --cov=app --cov-report=html
open htmlcov/index.html

docs.title: Running Specific Tests
docs.text:
# Specific test file
pytest tests/test_routes.py -v

# Specific test function
pytest tests/test_routes.py::test_index -v

# Tests matching pattern
pytest -k "test_endpoint" -v

docs.title: Test Output Options
docs.text:
# Verbose output
pytest tests/ -vv

# Show local variables on failure
pytest tests/ -l

# Stop on first failure
pytest tests/ -x

# Show slowest tests
pytest tests/ --durations=10

docs.title: Test Organization
docs.text:
tests/
├── __init__.py
├── conftest.py              # Fixtures and test configuration
├── test_models.py           # Database model tests
├── test_routes.py           # HTTP route tests
├── test_health_checker.py   # Health check logic tests
└── test_soap_utils.py       # SOAP utility tests

docs.title: Test Fixtures
docs.text:
Fixtures are defined in conftest.py:

@pytest.fixture
def app():
    """Create application for testing"""
    app = create_app('testing')
    with app.app_context():
        db.create_all()
        yield app
        db.session.remove()
        db.drop_all()

@pytest.fixture
def client(app):
    """Test client for making requests"""
    return app.test_client()

@pytest.fixture
def runner(app):
    """CLI test runner"""
    return app.test_cli_runner()

docs.title: Unit Test Example
docs.text:
def test_endpoint_to_dict():
    """Test Endpoint.to_dict() serialization"""
    endpoint = Endpoint(
        name="Test API",
        url="https://api.example.com/health",
        endpoint_type="REST",
        check_interval=60,
        timeout=30
    )

    result = endpoint.to_dict()

    assert result['name'] == "Test API"
    assert result['url'] == "https://api.example.com/health"
    assert result['endpoint_type'] == "REST"
    assert result['check_interval'] == 60

docs.title: Integration Test Example
docs.text:
def test_add_endpoint_workflow(client):
    """Test complete add endpoint workflow"""
    # Submit form
    response = client.post('/endpoints/add', data={
        'name': 'GitHub Status',
        'url': 'https://www.githubstatus.com/api/v2/status.json',
        'endpoint_type': 'REST',
        'check_interval': '300',
        'timeout': '30',
        'enabled': 'on'
    }, follow_redirects=True)

    # Check redirect
    assert response.status_code == 200

    # Verify database
    endpoint = Endpoint.query.filter_by(name='GitHub Status').first()
    assert endpoint is not None
    assert endpoint.url == 'https://www.githubstatus.com/api/v2/status.json'
    assert endpoint.enabled == True

docs.title: Mocking External Services
docs.text:
Use responses library for HTTP mocking:

import responses

@responses.activate
def test_health_check_success():
    """Test successful health check"""
    # Mock HTTP response
    responses.add(
        responses.GET,
        'https://api.example.com/health',
        json={'status': 'ok'},
        status=200
    )

    # Create test endpoint
    endpoint = Endpoint(
        name="Test",
        url="https://api.example.com/health",
        endpoint_type="REST"
    )

    # Perform check
    result = check_endpoint_health(endpoint)

    # Verify
    assert result.status == 'success'
    assert result.status_code == 200
    assert result.response_time > 0

docs.title: Coverage Goals
docs.text:
• Minimum: 70% overall coverage
  • Target: 80%+ overall coverage
  • Critical paths: 90%+ coverage

docs.title: Checking Coverage
docs.text:
# Generate coverage report
pytest tests/ --cov=app --cov-report=term-missing

# Show missing lines
pytest tests/ --cov=app --cov-report=term-missing

# HTML report with highlighting
pytest tests/ --cov=app --cov-report=html

docs.title: Coverage Configuration
docs.text:
Create .coveragerc:

[run]
source = app
omit = 
    */tests/*
    */venv/*
    */__pycache__/*

[report]
exclude_lines =
    pragma: no cover
    def __repr__
    raise AssertionError
    raise NotImplementedError
    if __name__ == .__main__.:

docs.title: Continuous Integration
docs.text:
Tests run automatically on GitHub Actions for:

  • Every push to main branch
  • Every pull request
  • Scheduled runs (optional)

docs.title: CI Configuration
docs.text: See .github/workflows/ci.yml for the complete configuration.

docs.title: SOAP Testing
docs.text:
For detailed SOAP testing procedures, see TESTING.md which includes:

  • VS Code task configurations
  • SOAP WSDL fetching tests
  • Payload generation tests
  • Integration test workflows
  • Debugging procedures

docs.title: 1. Test Naming
docs.text:
Use descriptive names that explain what is being tested:

# Good
def test_endpoint_creation_with_valid_data():
    pass

def test_health_check_timeout_handling():
    pass

# Bad
def test1():
    pass

def test_endpoint():
    pass

docs.title: 2. Arrange-Act-Assert Pattern
docs.text:
Structure tests clearly:

def test_something():
    # Arrange - Set up test data
    endpoint = create_test_endpoint()

    # Act - Perform the action
    result = check_endpoint_health(endpoint)

    # Assert - Verify the result
    assert result.status == 'success'

docs.title: 3. Test Independence
docs.text:
Each test should be independent:

# Good - Creates own data
def test_endpoint_creation():
    endpoint = Endpoint(name="Test")
    db.session.add(endpoint)
    db.session.commit()
    assert endpoint.id is not None

# Bad - Depends on other tests
def test_endpoint_exists():
    endpoint = Endpoint.query.first()  # Assumes data exists
    assert endpoint is not None

docs.title: 4. Use Fixtures
docs.text:
Reuse common setup with fixtures:

@pytest.fixture
def test_endpoint():
    """Create a test endpoint"""
    endpoint = Endpoint(
        name="Test API",
        url="https://api.example.com",
        endpoint_type="REST"
    )
    db.session.add(endpoint)
    db.session.commit()
    return endpoint

def test_with_endpoint(test_endpoint):
    """Test using the fixture"""
    assert test_endpoint.name == "Test API"

docs.title: 5. Test Edge Cases
docs.text:
Don't just test the happy path:

def test_health_check_with_invalid_url():
    """Test health check with malformed URL"""
    endpoint = Endpoint(url="not-a-url")
    result = check_endpoint_health(endpoint)
    assert result.status == 'failure'

def test_health_check_with_timeout():
    """Test health check that times out"""
    endpoint = Endpoint(url="https://httpstat.us/200?sleep=60000", timeout=1)
    result = check_endpoint_health(endpoint)
    assert result.status == 'timeout'

docs.title: Print Debugging
docs.text:
def test_something(client):
    response = client.get('/')
    print(response.data)  # Use -s flag: pytest -s
    assert response.status_code == 200

docs.title: Using pdb
docs.text:
def test_something():
    import pdb; pdb.set_trace()
    # Test code here

docs.title: VS Code Debugging
docs.text:
1. Set breakpoint in test
  2. Run → Debug Test
  3. Step through code

docs.title: Measuring Test Duration
docs.text:
# Show slowest 10 tests
pytest tests/ --durations=10

# Show all test durations
pytest tests/ --durations=0

docs.title: Benchmarking
docs.text:
For performance-critical code:

import time

def test_health_check_performance():
    """Ensure health check completes quickly"""
    endpoint = create_test_endpoint()

    start = time.time()
    result = check_endpoint_health(endpoint)
    duration = time.time() - start

    assert duration < 5.0  # Should complete in < 5 seconds

docs.title: What to Test
docs.text:
✅ Do test: - Public API functions - Database models - HTTP routes - Business logic - Error handling - Edge cases

❌ Don't test: - Third-party libraries - Framework internals - Obvious getters/setters

docs.title: When to Write Tests
docs.text:
1. Before coding (TDD)
  2. While coding (alongside features)
  3. After coding (bug fixes)
  4. When fixing bugs (regression tests)

docs.title: Tests Fail Locally
docs.text:
# Clear pytest cache
pytest --cache-clear

# Reset database
rm -f instance/test.db

# Reinstall dependencies
pip install -r requirements-dev.txt

docs.title: Database Locked
docs.text:
# Ensure no other instances running
pkill -f pytest

# Remove test database
rm -f instance/test.db

docs.title: Import Errors
docs.text:
# Verify virtual environment
which python

# Reinstall app
pip install -e .

docs.title: Configuration Guide
docs.text: This guide covers all configuration options available in App Health Monitor.

docs.title: Configuration Methods
docs.text:
App Health Monitor can be configured through:

  1. Environment variables (recommended for production)
  2. .env file (recommended for development)
  3. config.py (for advanced customization)

docs.title: SECRET_KEY
docs.text:
Required in production

Flask secret key for session management and CSRF protection.

SECRET_KEY=your-very-secret-random-key-here

Security Critical

Never use the default secret key in production! Generate a strong random key:

import secrets
print(secrets.token_hex(32))

docs.title: DATABASE_URL
docs.text:
Database connection string.

Default: sqlite:///instance/health_monitor.db

# SQLite (default)
DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///instance/health_monitor.db

# PostgreSQL
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:password@localhost/health_monitor

# MySQL
DATABASE_URL=mysql+pymysql://user:password@localhost/health_monitor

docs.title: FLASK_ENV
docs.text:
Application environment mode.

Default: development

Options: development, production, testing

FLASK_ENV=production

Effects:

  • development: Debug mode ON, auto-reload ON
  • production: Debug mode OFF, optimized settings
  • testing: Special test configuration

docs.title: PORT
docs.text:
Port number for the web server.

Default: 5000

PORT=8080

docs.title: HOST
docs.text:
Host address to bind to.

Default: 127.0.0.1 (localhost only)

# Listen on all interfaces (required for Docker)
HOST=0.0.0.0

# Localhost only
HOST=127.0.0.1

docs.title: DEFAULT_CHECK_INTERVAL
docs.text:
Default interval (in seconds) for health checks.

Default: 60

DEFAULT_CHECK_INTERVAL=300  # 5 minutes

docs.title: DEFAULT_TIMEOUT
docs.text:
Default timeout (in seconds) for HTTP requests.

Default: 30

DEFAULT_TIMEOUT=60

docs.title: SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI
docs.text:
Alternative to DATABASE_URL. If both are set, SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI takes precedence.

SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI=postgresql://user:password@localhost/health_monitor

docs.title: SQLALCHEMY_TRACK_MODIFICATIONS
docs.text:
Track object modifications.

Default: False (recommended)

SQLALCHEMY_TRACK_MODIFICATIONS=False

docs.title: SQLALCHEMY_ECHO
docs.text:
Log all SQL statements (useful for debugging).

Default: False

SQLALCHEMY_ECHO=True  # Enable SQL logging

docs.title: LOG_LEVEL
docs.text:
Application logging level.

Default: INFO

Options: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL

LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG

docs.title: MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH
docs.text:
Maximum size for request bodies (in bytes).

Default: 16777216 (16 MB)

MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH=52428800  # 50 MB

docs.title: Configuration File (.env)
docs.text:
Create a .env file in the project root:

# Copy sample configuration
cp config.sample.env .env

docs.title: Sample .env File
docs.text:
# =============================================================================
# App Health Monitor Configuration
# =============================================================================

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Security Settings
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# IMPORTANT: Change this to a strong random secret key in production!
SECRET_KEY=change-this-to-a-strong-random-secret-key

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Database Configuration
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# SQLite (default - good for development and small deployments)
DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///instance/health_monitor.db

# PostgreSQL (recommended for production)
# DATABASE_URL=postgresql://username:password@localhost:5432/health_monitor

# MySQL
# DATABASE_URL=mysql+pymysql://username:password@localhost:3306/health_monitor

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Application Settings
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Flask environment: development, production, or testing
FLASK_ENV=production

# Server port
PORT=5000

# Server host (0.0.0.0 for all interfaces, 127.0.0.1 for localhost only)
HOST=0.0.0.0

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Health Check Defaults
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Default interval between health checks (seconds)
DEFAULT_CHECK_INTERVAL=60

# Default timeout for HTTP requests (seconds)
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT=30

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Database Settings
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Disable modification tracking for better performance
SQLALCHEMY_TRACK_MODIFICATIONS=False

# Enable SQL query logging (useful for debugging)
# SQLALCHEMY_ECHO=False

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Logging
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Log level: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL
LOG_LEVEL=INFO

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Advanced Settings
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Maximum request body size (bytes)
MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH=16777216

docs.title: Development Configuration
docs.text:
For local development:

# .env.development
FLASK_ENV=development
SECRET_KEY=dev-secret-key-not-for-production
DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///instance/dev.db
PORT=5000
HOST=127.0.0.1
LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG
SQLALCHEMY_ECHO=True

Start with development config:

cp .env.development .env
python run.py

docs.title: Production Configuration
docs.text:
For production deployments:

# .env.production
FLASK_ENV=production
SECRET_KEY=<generate-strong-random-key>
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://healthmon:SecurePass123@db-server:5432/health_monitor
PORT=5000
HOST=0.0.0.0
LOG_LEVEL=WARNING
SQLALCHEMY_TRACK_MODIFICATIONS=False
DEFAULT_CHECK_INTERVAL=300
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT=60

docs.title: Testing Configuration
docs.text:
For automated testing:

# .env.testing
FLASK_ENV=testing
SECRET_KEY=test-secret-key
DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///:memory:
LOG_LEVEL=ERROR

docs.title: Endpoint-Specific Configuration
docs.text: When adding endpoints, you can configure:

docs.title: REST Endpoints
docs.text:
{
    "name": "Production API",
    "url": "https://api.example.com/health",
    "endpoint_type": "REST",
    "check_interval": 60,      # Check every 60 seconds
    "timeout": 30,              # 30-second timeout
    "enabled": True,
    "validation_enabled": False
}

docs.title: SOAP/WCF Endpoints
docs.text:
{
    "name": "Legacy SOAP Service",
    "url": "https://soap.example.com/service",
    "endpoint_type": "SOAP",
    "soap_action": "http://example.com/GetData",
    "soap_payload": "<?xml version=\"1.0\"?>...",
    "check_interval": 300,     # Check every 5 minutes
    "timeout": 60,
    "enabled": True
}

docs.title: No Authentication
docs.text:
{
    "auth_type": None
}

docs.title: Basic Authentication
docs.text:
{
    "auth_type": "Basic",
    "auth_username": "api_user",
    "auth_password": "api_password"
}

Password Security

Passwords are currently stored in plaintext. Use environment-specific credentials and consider implementing encryption (see Security Hardening).

docs.title: Windows/Kerberos Authentication
docs.text:
{
    "auth_type": "Windows"  # or "Kerberos"
}

Requires additional configuration:

# Install Kerberos tools
pip install requests-kerberos

docs.title: Contains Validation
docs.text:
Check if response contains specific text:

{
    "validation_enabled": True,
    "validation_type": "contains",
    "expected_content": "healthy"
}

docs.title: Equals Validation
docs.text:
Check if response exactly matches:

{
    "validation_enabled": True,
    "validation_type": "equals",
    "expected_content": "OK"
}

docs.title: Regex Validation
docs.text:
Check if response matches a pattern:

{
    "validation_enabled": True,
    "validation_type": "regex",
    "expected_content": "^status:\\s*ok$"
}

docs.title: JSON Path Validation
docs.text:
Check specific JSON fields:

{
    "validation_enabled": True,
    "validation_type": "json_path",
    "expected_content": "$.status=healthy"
}

docs.title: docker-compose.yml
docs.text:
Configure Docker Compose deployment:

version: '3.8'

services:
  health-monitor:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "5000:5000"
    environment:
      - SECRET_KEY=${SECRET_KEY}
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://healthmon:password@db:5432/health_monitor
      - FLASK_ENV=production
      - PORT=5000
      - HOST=0.0.0.0
    volumes:
      - ./instance:/app/instance
    depends_on:
      - db
    restart: unless-stopped

  db:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_DB=health_monitor
      - POSTGRES_USER=healthmon
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=password
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  postgres_data:

docs.title: Environment Variables in Docker
docs.text:
Pass via .env file:

# .env
SECRET_KEY=your-secret-key

Or via docker-compose:

docker-compose up -d

Or via docker run:

docker run -d \
  -e SECRET_KEY=your-secret-key \
  -e DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///instance/health_monitor.db \
  -e FLASK_ENV=production \
  -p 5000:5000 \
  health-monitor

docs.title: 1. Use Environment-Specific Configs
docs.text:
Keep separate configuration files for each environment:

.env.development
.env.staging
.env.production

docs.title: 2. Never Commit Secrets
docs.text:
Add .env to .gitignore:

.env
.env.*
!.env.example

docs.title: 3. Use Strong Secret Keys
docs.text:
Generate cryptographically secure secret keys:

import secrets
print(secrets.token_hex(32))

docs.title: 4. Use Environment Variables in Production
docs.text:
Don't use .env files in production. Set environment variables directly:

# systemd service
Environment="SECRET_KEY=xxx"
Environment="DATABASE_URL=postgresql://..."

# Docker
docker run -e SECRET_KEY=xxx -e DATABASE_URL=xxx ...

# Kubernetes
envFrom:
  - secretRef:
      name: health-monitor-secrets

docs.title: 5. Validate Configuration on Startup
docs.text:
The application validates critical configuration on startup:

  • SECRET_KEY must be set in production
  • DATABASE_URL must be valid
  • Port must be available

docs.title: Configuration Not Loading
docs.text:
Check that .env file is in the correct location:

# Must be in project root
App-health-monitor/
├── .env          <-- Here
├── app/
├── run.py
└── ...

docs.title: Environment Variables Not Working
docs.text:
Verify environment variables are set:

echo $SECRET_KEY
echo $DATABASE_URL

docs.title: Database Connection Errors
docs.text:
Test database connection:

from app.app import create_app
app = create_app()
with app.app_context():
    from app.models import db
    db.create_all()
    print("Database connected successfully!")

docs.title: Port Already in Use
docs.text:
Change the port:

PORT=8080 python run.py

docs.title: Using the Dashboard
docs.text: The App Health Monitor dashboard provides a real-time view of all your monitored endpoints with their current health status, performance metrics, and historical data.

docs.title: Dashboard Overview
docs.text: The dashboard is the main interface of the application, accessible at http://localhost:5000/.

docs.title: Main Components
docs.text:
1. Navigation Bar - Access different sections of the application
  2. Endpoint Cards - Visual representation of each monitored endpoint
  3. Quick Actions - Add, edit, delete, and check endpoints
  4. Status Indicators - Visual health status indicators

docs.title: Endpoint Cards
docs.text: Each endpoint is displayed as a card showing:

docs.title: Status Information
docs.text:
• Name - The friendly name you assigned
  • URL - The endpoint being monitored
  • Type - REST, SOAP, WCF, or GraphQL
  • Status Badge - Visual indicator of current health:
  • 🟢 Healthy - Last check was successful (HTTP 2xx)
  • 🔴 Failed - Last check failed (HTTP 4xx, 5xx)
  • 🟡 Timeout - Request timed out
  • ⚠️ Validation Failed - Response validation failed
  • ⚪ Not Checked Yet - No health checks performed

docs.title: Performance Metrics
docs.text:
• Response Time - Latest response time in milliseconds
  • Status Code - HTTP status code from the last check
  • Last Checked - Timestamp of the most recent health check
  • Error Message - Displayed if the check failed

docs.title: Statistics (Available in Logs View)
docs.text:
• Uptime % - Percentage of successful checks
  • Total Checks - Number of health checks performed
  • Success Count - Number of successful checks
  • Average Response Time - Mean response time across all checks

docs.title: Add Endpoint
docs.text:
Click the "Add Endpoint" button to create a new monitored endpoint.

Required Fields: - Name - URL - Type (REST, SOAP, WCF, GraphQL)

Optional Fields: - Check Interval (default: 60 seconds) - Timeout (default: 30 seconds) - Authentication settings - Response validation rules

See Managing Endpoints for detailed information.

docs.title: Manual Check
docs.text:
Click "Check Now" on any endpoint card to immediately trigger a health check without waiting for the scheduled interval.

Use cases: - Verify a fix after an outage - Test a newly added endpoint - Get immediate status update

docs.title: Edit Endpoint
docs.text:
Click "Edit" to modify endpoint configuration:

  • Change check interval
  • Update URL or authentication
  • Enable/disable the endpoint
  • Modify validation rules

docs.title: Delete Endpoint
docs.text:
Click "Delete" to remove an endpoint. This will:

  • Stop scheduled health checks
  • Remove the endpoint from the dashboard
  • Delete all associated health check history

Deletion is Permanent

Deleting an endpoint removes all historical data. This action cannot be undone.

docs.title: View Logs
docs.text:
Click "View Logs" to see the health check history for a specific endpoint.

Logs include: - Timestamp of each check - Status and status code - Response time - Error messages - Validation results

docs.title: Auto Refresh
docs.text:
The dashboard automatically updates the health status of endpoints as checks are performed in the background.

Refresh interval: Based on individual endpoint check intervals

docs.title: Color-Coded Status
docs.text:
Quick visual identification of endpoint health:

Color Status Meaning Green ✅ Healthy Endpoint is responding successfully Red ❌ Failed Endpoint returned an error Yellow ⏱️ Timeout Request exceeded timeout threshold Orange ⚠️ Validation Failed Response received but validation failed Gray ⚪ Pending No checks performed yet

docs.title: Responsive Design
docs.text:
The dashboard is mobile-friendly and adapts to different screen sizes:

  • Desktop - Multi-column grid layout
  • Tablet - Two-column layout
  • Mobile - Single-column layout

docs.title: Main Menu
docs.text:
• Dashboard (/) - Main endpoint overview
  • Add Endpoint (/endpoints/add) - Create new endpoint
  • All Logs (/logs) - View all health check logs
  • API (/api/health) - JSON API endpoint

docs.title: Breadcrumbs
docs.text: Navigate back to previous pages using breadcrumb navigation at the top of each page.

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text:
Speed up your workflow with keyboard shortcuts:

Shortcut Action A Add new endpoint L View all logs R Refresh dashboard ? Show help

Future Feature

Keyboard shortcuts are planned for a future release.

docs.title: Sorting
docs.text:
Endpoints can be sorted by: - Name (alphabetical) - Status (healthy first or failed first) - Last checked (most recent first) - Response time (fastest first)

Future Feature

Custom sorting is planned for a future release. Currently, endpoints are displayed in creation order.

docs.title: Filtering
docs.text:
Filter endpoints by: - Type (REST, SOAP, WCF, GraphQL) - Status (Healthy, Failed, Timeout) - Tags (when implemented)

Future Feature

Filtering is planned for a future release.

docs.title: 1. Use Descriptive Names
docs.text:
Give endpoints clear, descriptive names:

✅ Good: - "Production API - User Service" - "Staging - Payment Gateway" - "Legacy SOAP - Order Processing"

❌ Avoid: - "API 1" - "Test" - "Endpoint"

docs.title: 2. Organize by Environment
docs.text:
Prefix endpoint names with the environment:

  • [PROD] Customer API
  • [STAGE] Payment Service
  • [DEV] Authentication

docs.title: 3. Set Appropriate Check Intervals
docs.text:
• Critical production endpoints: 30-60 seconds
  • Standard endpoints: 5 minutes
  • Non-critical or slow endpoints: 15-30 minutes

docs.title: 4. Use Validation
docs.text:
Enable response validation to catch issues beyond HTTP status codes:

  • Validate critical response fields
  • Check for expected content
  • Ensure JSON structure is correct

docs.title: 5. Monitor the Dashboard Regularly
docs.text:
• Check daily for any red/yellow indicators
  • Review error messages for failed checks
  • Adjust check intervals based on usage patterns

docs.title: Endpoint Stuck on "Pending"
docs.text:
Causes: - Scheduler not running - Endpoint is disabled - Check interval is very long

Solutions: 1. Check that the application is running 2. Verify endpoint is enabled (Edit > Enable checkbox) 3. Manually trigger a check with "Check Now" 4. Check application logs for errors

docs.title: Dashboard Not Updating
docs.text:
Causes: - Browser cache - JavaScript errors - Network issues

Solutions: 1. Hard refresh (Ctrl+F5 or Cmd+Shift+R) 2. Clear browser cache 3. Check browser console for errors 4. Try a different browser

docs.title: Wrong Status Displayed
docs.text:
Causes: - Recent configuration change - Manual check overriding scheduled check - Validation rules changed

Solutions: 1. Wait for next scheduled check 2. Trigger a manual check 3. Review validation rules in endpoint settings

docs.title: Performance Issues
docs.text:
Causes: - Too many endpoints - Very short check intervals - Slow monitored endpoints

Solutions: 1. Increase check intervals 2. Disable non-critical endpoints 3. Increase timeout values 4. Use more powerful server hardware

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Managing Endpoints - Learn about endpoint configuration
  • Viewing Logs - Deep dive into health check history
  • API Reference - Programmatic access to health data

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text: This guide provides detailed installation instructions for App Health Monitor across different environments and platforms.

docs.title: Minimum Requirements
docs.text:
• Python: 3.12 or higher
  • RAM: 512 MB minimum, 1 GB recommended
  • Disk Space: 100 MB for application + database storage
  • Operating System: Linux, macOS, or Windows

docs.title: Network Requirements
docs.text:
• Outbound HTTPS access to monitored endpoints
  • Inbound access on configured port (default: 5000)

docs.title: Method 1: Standard Python Installation
docs.text: This is the recommended method for most users.

docs.title: 1. Install Python
docs.text:
Ubuntu/DebianmacOSWindows 
sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3.12 python3.12-venv python3-pip

brew install python@3.12

Download and install Python 3.12 from python.org

Make sure to check "Add Python to PATH" during installation.

docs.title: 2. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/mgboyle/App-health-monitor.git
cd App-health-monitor

docs.title: 3. Create Virtual Environment
docs.text:
Linux/macOSWindows 
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate

python -m venv venv
venv\Scripts\activate

docs.title: 4. Install Dependencies
docs.text: pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 5. Verify Installation
docs.text:
python run.py

Access the application at http://localhost:5000.

docs.title: Method 2: Docker Installation
docs.text: Docker provides isolated, reproducible deployments.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Docker 20.10 or higher
  • Docker Compose 2.0 or higher (optional)

docs.title: Option A: Docker Compose (Recommended)
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/mgboyle/App-health-monitor.git
cd App-health-monitor

# Start the application
docker-compose up -d

# View logs
docker-compose logs -f

# Stop the application
docker-compose down

docs.title: Option B: Docker CLI
docs.text:
# Build the image
docker build -t health-monitor .

# Run the container
docker run -d \
  -p 5000:5000 \
  -v $(pwd)/instance:/app/instance \
  -e SECRET_KEY=your-secret-key-here \
  --name health-monitor \
  health-monitor

# View logs
docker logs -f health-monitor

# Stop the container
docker stop health-monitor
docker rm health-monitor

docs.title: Method 3: Development Installation
docs.text: For contributors and developers.

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/mgboyle/App-health-monitor.git
cd App-health-monitor

docs.title: 2. Create Virtual Environment
docs.text:
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: venv\Scripts\activate

docs.title: 3. Install Development Dependencies
docs.text:
pip install -r requirements-dev.txt

This includes testing tools (pytest) and documentation tools (mkdocs).

docs.title: 4. Run Tests
docs.text: pytest tests/ -v

docs.title: 5. Run the Application
docs.text:
python run.py

See the Development Setup Guide for more details.

docs.title: 1. Configure Environment Variables
docs.text:
Create a .env file in the project root:

cp config.sample.env .env

Edit .env with your preferred settings:

# Security
SECRET_KEY=generate-a-random-secret-key-here

# Database
DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///instance/health_monitor.db

# Application Settings
DEFAULT_CHECK_INTERVAL=60
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT=30
PORT=5000

# Flask Settings
FLASK_ENV=production

Security: Secret Key

Never use the default secret key in production! Generate a strong random key:

import secrets
print(secrets.token_hex(32))

docs.title: 2. Initialize the Database
docs.text:
The database is automatically created on first run. To manually initialize:

python -c "from app.app import create_app; from app.models import db; app = create_app(); app.app_context().push(); db.create_all()"

docs.title: 3. (Optional) Seed Sample Data
docs.text:
For testing purposes, you can add sample endpoints:

python seed_data.py

docs.title: 4. Configure Systemd Service (Linux)
docs.text:
For production deployments on Linux, create a systemd service:

# /etc/systemd/system/health-monitor.service
[Unit]
Description=App Health Monitor
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=www-data
WorkingDirectory=/opt/health-monitor
Environment="PATH=/opt/health-monitor/venv/bin"
ExecStart=/opt/health-monitor/venv/bin/python run.py
Restart=always
RestartSec=10

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Enable and start the service:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable health-monitor
sudo systemctl start health-monitor
sudo systemctl status health-monitor

docs.title: SQLite (Default)
docs.text:
SQLite is the default and requires no additional setup. Perfect for:

  • Development
  • Small deployments
  • Single-server setups
  • Quick testing

Database file location: instance/health_monitor.db

docs.title: PostgreSQL (Production)
docs.text: For production deployments with multiple instances, use PostgreSQL:

docs.title: 1. Install PostgreSQL
docs.text:
Ubuntu/DebianmacOS 
sudo apt install postgresql postgresql-contrib

brew install postgresql

docs.title: 2. Create Database
docs.text:
sudo -u postgres psql

CREATE DATABASE health_monitor;
CREATE USER healthmon WITH PASSWORD 'strong-password';
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE health_monitor TO healthmon;
\q

docs.title: 3. Install Python Driver
docs.text: pip install psycopg2-binary

docs.title: 4. Update Configuration
docs.text:
In .env:

DATABASE_URL=postgresql://healthmon:strong-password@localhost/health_monitor

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: After installation, verify everything is working:

docs.title: 1. Check Application Status
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:5000/

Should return the HTML dashboard.

docs.title: 2. Check API
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:5000/api/health

Should return JSON health status.

docs.title: 3. Add a Test Endpoint
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:5000/endpoints/add \
  -F "name=GitHub Status" \
  -F "url=https://www.githubstatus.com/api/v2/status.json" \
  -F "endpoint_type=REST" \
  -F "check_interval=300" \
  -F "timeout=30" \
  -F "enabled=on"

docs.title: 4. View Logs
docs.text:
Check that health checks are running:

# View application logs (if using systemd)
journalctl -u health-monitor -f

# Or check the web interface
open http://localhost:5000/logs

docs.title: Upgrading
docs.text: To upgrade to the latest version:

docs.title: Standard Installation
docs.text:
cd App-health-monitor
git pull origin main
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt --upgrade
python run.py

docs.title: Docker Installation
docs.text:
cd App-health-monitor
git pull origin main
docker-compose down
docker-compose build
docker-compose up -d

docs.title: Standard Installation
docs.text:
# Stop the application (Ctrl+C if running in foreground)

# Stop systemd service (if configured)
sudo systemctl stop health-monitor
sudo systemctl disable health-monitor
sudo rm /etc/systemd/system/health-monitor.service

# Remove files
cd ..
rm -rf App-health-monitor

docs.title: Docker Installation
docs.text:
docker-compose down -v  # -v removes volumes
cd ..
rm -rf App-health-monitor

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Configuration Guide - Configure the application
  • Quick Start - Get started quickly
  • Managing Endpoints - Add and configure endpoints
  • Production Deployment - Deploy to production

docs.title: Python Version Issues
docs.text:
# Check Python version
python --version

# If using multiple Python versions
python3.12 --version

docs.title: Permission Errors
docs.text:
Linux/macOSWindows 
# Use virtual environment (recommended)
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

Run Command Prompt or PowerShell as Administrator.

docs.title: Dependency Conflicts
docs.text:
# Clear pip cache
pip cache purge

# Reinstall in clean environment
rm -rf venv
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Docker Issues
docs.text:
# Check Docker is running
docker --version
docker ps

# Rebuild without cache
docker-compose build --no-cache

# View detailed logs
docker-compose logs -f

For more help, see the Troubleshooting Guide.

docs.title: Viewing Logs
docs.text: App Health Monitor maintains a complete history of all health checks performed. This guide explains how to view, analyze, and use the logging features.

docs.title: Endpoint-Specific Logs
docs.text:
View logs for a single endpoint:

Via Web UI: 1. Click "View Logs" on any endpoint card 2. Or navigate to /endpoints/{id}/logs

Via API: 

curl http://localhost:5000/api/endpoints/1/checks?limit=100

docs.title: All Logs
docs.text:
View logs for all endpoints:

Via Web UI: 1. Click "All Logs" in the navigation menu 2. Or navigate to /logs

docs.title: Log Information
docs.text: Each log entry contains:

docs.title: Basic Information
docs.text:
• Timestamp - When the check was performed
  • Endpoint Name - Which endpoint was checked
  • Status - Result of the check
  • Status Code - HTTP status code received
  • Response Time - How long the request took (ms)

docs.title: Status Values
docs.text: Status Meaning success Request completed successfully (HTTP 2xx) failure Request failed (HTTP 4xx/5xx or network error) timeout Request exceeded timeout threshold validation_failed Response received but validation failed

docs.title: Additional Details
docs.text:
• Error Message - Error details if the check failed
  • Validation Error - Specific validation failure message
  • URL - The endpoint that was checked

docs.title: Statistics Panel
docs.text: The logs view includes a statistics panel showing:

docs.title: Uptime Percentage
docs.text:
Percentage of successful health checks.

Calculation: 

Uptime % = (Successful Checks / Total Checks) × 100

Example: - Total Checks: 1000 - Successful: 985 - Uptime: 98.5%

docs.title: Total Checks
docs.text: Total number of health checks performed for the endpoint.

docs.title: Success Count
docs.text: Number of successful health checks.

docs.title: Average Response Time
docs.text:
Mean response time across all health checks (in milliseconds).

Note: Only includes successful checks in the calculation.

docs.title: By Time Range
docs.text:
Filter logs to a specific time period:

Future Feature

Time range filtering is planned for a future release. Currently, logs show the most recent entries.

Planned filters: - Last hour - Last 24 hours - Last 7 days - Last 30 days - Custom date range

docs.title: By Status
docs.text:
Filter by check result:

Future Feature

Status filtering is planned for a future release.

Planned filters: - Success only - Failures only - Timeouts only - Validation failures only

docs.title: By Endpoint
docs.text:
When viewing "All Logs", filter by specific endpoint.

Future Feature

Endpoint filtering is planned for a future release.

docs.title: Pagination
docs.text:
Logs are paginated to improve performance:

  • Default page size: 50 entries
  • Navigation: Previous/Next buttons
  • Limit parameter: Customize via API

API Example: 

# Get 100 most recent checks
curl http://localhost:5000/api/endpoints/1/checks?limit=100

# Get next page
curl http://localhost:5000/api/endpoints/1/checks?limit=50&page=2

docs.title: JSON Export (API)
docs.text:
Export logs in JSON format:

# Export specific endpoint logs
curl http://localhost:5000/api/endpoints/1/checks?limit=1000 > logs.json

# Export all endpoint data
curl http://localhost:5000/api/health > health_status.json

docs.title: CSV Export
docs.text:
Future Feature

CSV export is planned for a future release.

Planned format: 

timestamp,endpoint_name,status,status_code,response_time_ms,error_message
2024-01-01 12:00:00,Production API,success,200,123.45,
2024-01-01 12:01:00,Production API,success,200,135.67,

docs.title: Identifying Patterns
docs.text:
Look for patterns in the logs:

Intermittent failures: - Check appears healthy most of the time - Occasional failures at specific times - May indicate resource constraints or scheduled jobs

Degrading performance: - Response times gradually increasing - May indicate memory leaks or resource exhaustion

Regular timeouts: - Consistent timeout errors - May indicate insufficient timeout setting or slow service

docs.title: Performance Trends
docs.text:
Monitor response time trends:

Healthy pattern: - Consistent response times - Low variation - Few outliers

Warning signs: - Increasing average response time - High variation in response times - Frequent spikes

docs.title: Uptime Analysis
docs.text:
Use uptime percentage to measure reliability:

SLA Examples: - 99.9% uptime - "Three nines" - 8.76 hours downtime/year - 99.95% uptime - 4.38 hours downtime/year - 99.99% uptime - "Four nines" - 52.56 minutes downtime/year

docs.title: 1. Investigating an Incident
docs.text:
When an endpoint fails:

  1. View endpoint-specific logs
  2. Look at the error message
  3. Check if validation failed
  4. Review response times before failure
  5. Check for patterns (time of day, frequency)

Example Investigation: 

Error: Connection refused
Pattern: Started at 2:00 AM
Analysis: Nightly maintenance window
Action: Adjust check schedule or disable during maintenance

docs.title: 2. Monitoring SLA Compliance
docs.text:
Track if services meet SLA requirements:

  1. Check uptime percentage
  2. Compare against SLA target (e.g., 99.9%)
  3. Review failure count
  4. Identify root causes of downtime

docs.title: 3. Performance Baselining
docs.text:
Establish normal performance baselines:

  1. Review average response time over 30 days
  2. Identify typical range
  3. Set alerts for values outside normal range
  4. Track improvements after optimizations

docs.title: 4. Capacity Planning
docs.text:
Use response time trends for capacity planning:

  1. Monitor response time growth
  2. Correlate with usage patterns
  3. Identify when scaling is needed
  4. Plan infrastructure upgrades

docs.title: 1. Regular Review
docs.text:
Review logs regularly:

  • Daily: Check for new failures
  • Weekly: Review uptime percentages
  • Monthly: Analyze trends and patterns

docs.title: 2. Act on Failures
docs.text:
Don't ignore failures:

  1. Investigate root cause
  2. Document issues
  3. Fix or adjust configuration
  4. Verify fix with manual check

docs.title: 3. Set Realistic Expectations
docs.text:
Understand that 100% uptime is unrealistic:

  • Network issues happen
  • Services restart
  • Maintenance windows occur

Target: 99%+ uptime for production services

docs.title: 4. Use Validation
docs.text:
Don't rely solely on HTTP status codes:

  • Enable response validation
  • Verify critical response fields
  • Catch degraded but "responding" services

docs.title: 5. Archive Old Logs
docs.text:
Plan for log retention:

Future Feature

Automatic log archival and retention policies are planned for a future release.

Recommendations: - Keep detailed logs for 30-90 days - Archive summary statistics for longer - Delete very old data to save space

docs.title: Logs Not Appearing
docs.text:
Causes: - Endpoint not enabled - Scheduler not running - Check interval too long - First check hasn't run yet

Solutions: 1. Verify endpoint is enabled 2. Check application is running 3. Manually trigger a check 4. Wait for next scheduled check

docs.title: Statistics Incorrect
docs.text:
Causes: - Recent configuration change - Database migration - Validation rules changed

Solutions: 1. Wait for more checks to accumulate 2. Manually recalculate if needed 3. Review recent configuration changes

docs.title: Missing Time Periods
docs.text:
Causes: - Application was stopped - Endpoint was disabled - System maintenance

Solutions: 1. Check application uptime logs 2. Review endpoint history (enabled/disabled) 3. Fill gaps with manual checks if needed

docs.title: Response Times Seem Wrong
docs.text:
Causes: - Network latency included - Server processing time varies - Validation adds overhead

Notes: - Response time includes full round-trip - DNS lookup time included - SSL handshake included - Network latency included

docs.title: Current Behavior
docs.text:
All health check logs are retained indefinitely.

Database Growth: - ~1KB per check - 1 endpoint @ 60s interval = ~1.4MB/day - 10 endpoints = ~14MB/day

docs.title: Future Features
docs.text:
Planned automatic retention policies:

  • Detailed logs: 90 days
  • Hourly summaries: 1 year
  • Daily summaries: Forever

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Troubleshooting - Solve common issues
  • API Reference - Programmatic access to logs
  • Dashboard - Real-time monitoring

docs.title: Managing Endpoints
docs.text: This guide covers everything you need to know about adding, configuring, and managing endpoints in App Health Monitor.

docs.title: Endpoint Types
docs.text: App Health Monitor supports multiple endpoint types:

docs.title: REST APIs
docs.text:
Standard REST/HTTP endpoints.

Use for: - RESTful web services - HTTP health check endpoints - JSON APIs - Simple HTTP GET/POST endpoints

Example: 

URL: https://api.example.com/health
Type: REST
Method: GET (default)

docs.title: SOAP Web Services
docs.text:
SOAP/XML-based web services.

Use for: - Legacy SOAP services - WSDL-based services - XML web services

Example: 

URL: https://soap.example.com/service
Type: SOAP
SOAP Action: http://example.com/GetStatus
Payload: <soap:Envelope>...</soap:Envelope>

docs.title: WCF Services
docs.text:
Windows Communication Foundation services.

Use for: - .NET WCF services - Enterprise Windows services

Example: 

URL: http://wcf.example.com/Service.svc
Type: WCF

docs.title: GraphQL APIs
docs.text:
GraphQL endpoints.

Use for: - GraphQL APIs - Flexible query-based services

Example: 

URL: https://api.example.com/graphql
Type: GraphQL
Query: { health { status } }

docs.title: Via Web Interface
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the dashboard
  2. Click "Add Endpoint"
  3. Fill in the required fields
  4. Configure optional settings
  5. Click "Add Endpoint"

docs.title: Via API
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:5000/endpoints/add \
  -F "name=My API" \
  -F "url=https://api.example.com/health" \
  -F "endpoint_type=REST" \
  -F "check_interval=60" \
  -F "timeout=30" \
  -F "enabled=on"

docs.title: Name
docs.text:
Required

A friendly, descriptive name for the endpoint.

Best practices: - Use clear, specific names - Include environment (PROD, STAGE, DEV) - Indicate the service purpose

Examples: - [PROD] User Service API - [STAGE] Payment Gateway - Legacy Orders SOAP Service

docs.title: URL
docs.text:
Required

The full URL of the endpoint to monitor.

Format: 

https://api.example.com/health
http://internal-service:8080/status
https://soap.example.com/Service.svc

Notes: - Must include protocol (http:// or https://) - Can include port numbers - Should point to a health check or status endpoint when available

docs.title: Type
docs.text:
Required

The protocol type of the endpoint.

Options: - REST - SOAP - WCF - GraphQL

docs.title: Check Interval
docs.text:
How often to perform health checks (in seconds).

Default: 60 seconds

Recommendations: - Critical production: 30-60 seconds - Standard services: 300 seconds (5 minutes) - Low priority: 900+ seconds (15+ minutes)

Example: 

60    # Check every minute
300   # Check every 5 minutes
3600  # Check every hour

docs.title: Timeout
docs.text:
Maximum time to wait for a response (in seconds).

Default: 30 seconds

Recommendations: - Fast APIs: 10-15 seconds - Standard: 30 seconds - Slow services: 60-120 seconds

Notes: - Should be less than check_interval - Longer timeouts use more resources

docs.title: Enabled
docs.text:
Whether to actively monitor this endpoint.

Default: Enabled

Use cases for disabling: - Temporary maintenance - Service is down for known reasons - Testing new configuration

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: App Health Monitor supports multiple authentication methods.

docs.title: No Authentication
docs.text:
For public endpoints that don't require authentication.

auth_type: None

docs.title: Basic Authentication
docs.text:
Username and password authentication.

auth_type: "Basic"
auth_username: "api_user"
auth_password: "api_password"

Example: 

curl -X POST http://localhost:5000/endpoints/add \
  -F "name=Authenticated API" \
  -F "url=https://api.example.com/secure" \
  -F "endpoint_type=REST" \
  -F "auth_type=Basic" \
  -F "auth_username=user123" \
  -F "auth_password=pass456"

Password Storage

Passwords are currently stored in plaintext in the database. Use service accounts with limited permissions. See Security Hardening for best practices.

docs.title: Windows Authentication
docs.text:
For Windows-integrated services.

auth_type: "Windows"

Requirements: - Kerberos configured - requests-kerberos installed

docs.title: Kerberos Authentication
docs.text:
For Kerberos-authenticated services.

auth_type: "Kerberos"

Requirements: - Kerberos tickets configured - requests-kerberos installed

docs.title: Response Validation
docs.text: Validate that endpoints not only respond, but respond correctly.

docs.title: Enable Validation
docs.text:
validation_enabled: True
validation_type: "contains"  # or "equals", "regex", "json_path"
expected_content: "healthy"

docs.title: Contains
docs.text:
Check if the response contains specific text.

Example: 

validation_type: "contains"
expected_content: "status: ok"

Use cases: - Simple text-based health checks - HTML page contains specific content - Log file contains success message

docs.title: Equals
docs.text:
Check if the response exactly matches expected content.

Example: 

validation_type: "equals"
expected_content: "OK"

Use cases: - Simple OK/FAIL responses - Fixed response format - Binary health indicators

docs.title: Regex
docs.text:
Match response against a regular expression.

Example: 

validation_type: "regex"
expected_content: "^status:\\s*(ok|healthy)$"

Use cases: - Pattern matching - Multiple valid responses - Complex text validation

docs.title: JSON Path
docs.text:
Validate specific JSON fields.

Example: 

validation_type: "json_path"
expected_content: "$.status=healthy"

Format: jsonpath=expected_value

Examples: 

$.status=healthy
$.data.health=ok
$.errors.length=0

Use cases: - JSON API responses - Validate specific fields - Check nested values

docs.title: SOAP-Specific Configuration
docs.text: For SOAP endpoints, additional fields are available:

docs.title: SOAP Action
docs.text:
The SOAP action/method to call.

Example: 

soap_action: "http://example.com/GetStatus"

docs.title: SOAP Payload
docs.text:
The XML request body.

Example: 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<soap:Envelope xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/">
  <soap:Body>
    <GetStatus xmlns="http://example.com/">
      <ServiceId>12345</ServiceId>
    </GetStatus>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>

Helper Tools:

The UI provides tools to help generate SOAP payloads:

  1. Fetch Operations - Parse WSDL to list available operations
  2. Generate Payload - Create sample SOAP envelope for an operation

docs.title: Via Web Interface
docs.text:
1. Click "Edit" on the endpoint card
  2. Modify the desired fields
  3. Click "Update Endpoint"

docs.title: Via API
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:5000/endpoints/1/edit \
  -F "name=Updated Name" \
  -F "check_interval=300"

docs.title: Via Web Interface
docs.text:
1. Click "Delete" on the endpoint card
  2. Confirm the deletion

docs.title: Via API
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:5000/endpoints/1/delete

Permanent Deletion

Deleting an endpoint removes all health check history. This cannot be undone.

docs.title: Temporarily Disabling Endpoints
docs.text:
Instead of deleting, you can disable endpoints:

  1. Edit the endpoint
  2. Uncheck "Enabled"
  3. Save changes

Disabled endpoints: - Stop scheduling health checks - Remain in the dashboard (grayed out) - Preserve historical data - Can be re-enabled later

docs.title: Manual Health Checks
docs.text: Trigger an immediate health check:

docs.title: Via Web Interface
docs.text: Click "Check Now" on the endpoint card.

docs.title: Via API
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:5000/endpoints/1/check

Use cases: - Test new configuration - Verify fix after incident - Force immediate status update

docs.title: 1. Start with Longer Intervals
docs.text:
Begin with 5-minute intervals and adjust based on needs:

check_interval: 300  # Start here

Increase frequency only for critical endpoints.

docs.title: 2. Set Realistic Timeouts
docs.text:
Match timeout to expected response time:

# Fast API
timeout: 15

# Slow service
timeout: 120

docs.title: 3. Use Validation for Critical Endpoints
docs.text:
Don't just check HTTP status - validate the response:

validation_enabled: True
validation_type: "json_path"
expected_content: "$.status=operational"

docs.title: 4. Organize with Naming Conventions
docs.text:
Use consistent naming:

[ENV] Service Name - Component

Examples: - [PROD] Payment API - Checkout - [STAGE] User Service - Auth

docs.title: 5. Monitor the Right Endpoints
docs.text:
Point to dedicated health check endpoints when available:

✅ Good: - https://api.example.com/health - https://api.example.com/status - https://api.example.com/v1/healthz

❌ Avoid: - Resource endpoints that perform operations - Endpoints that modify data - User-facing pages

docs.title: 6. Use Service Accounts
docs.text:
For authenticated endpoints, create dedicated monitoring accounts with:

  • Read-only access
  • Minimal permissions
  • Separate from user accounts

docs.title: Pattern 1: Simple REST Health Check
docs.text:
{
  "name": "[PROD] API Health",
  "url": "https://api.example.com/health",
  "endpoint_type": "REST",
  "check_interval": 60,
  "timeout": 10,
  "validation_enabled": true,
  "validation_type": "json_path",
  "expected_content": "$.status=ok"
}

docs.title: Pattern 2: Authenticated API
docs.text:
{
  "name": "[PROD] Secure API",
  "url": "https://api.example.com/secure/health",
  "endpoint_type": "REST",
  "auth_type": "Basic",
  "auth_username": "monitor",
  "auth_password": "secret",
  "check_interval": 120,
  "timeout": 30
}

docs.title: Pattern 3: SOAP Service
docs.text:
{
  "name": "Legacy Order Service",
  "url": "https://soap.example.com/OrderService.svc",
  "endpoint_type": "SOAP",
  "soap_action": "http://example.com/GetServiceStatus",
  "soap_payload": "<?xml version='1.0'?>...",
  "check_interval": 300,
  "timeout": 60
}

docs.title: Pattern 4: GraphQL API
docs.text:
{
  "name": "[PROD] GraphQL API",
  "url": "https://api.example.com/graphql",
  "endpoint_type": "GraphQL",
  "check_interval": 60,
  "timeout": 30,
  "validation_enabled": true,
  "validation_type": "contains",
  "expected_content": "\"healthy\""
}

docs.title: Endpoint Always Failing
docs.text:
Check: 1. URL is correct and accessible 2. Authentication credentials are valid 3. Timeout is sufficient 4. Validation rules are correct 5. Network connectivity

Debug: 

# Test manually with curl
curl -v https://api.example.com/health

# Check with auth
curl -v -u username:password https://api.example.com/health

docs.title: Timeout Issues
docs.text: Solutions: 1. Increase timeout value 2. Check network latency 3. Verify endpoint performance 4. Consider if endpoint is too slow for monitoring

docs.title: Validation Failures
docs.text:
Check: 1. Response format matches expected 2. JSON path is correct 3. Regex pattern is valid 4. Case sensitivity

Debug: View the actual response in logs to see what was returned.

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: Get App Health Monitor up and running in just a few minutes!

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed:

  • Python 3.12 or higher
  • pip (Python package manager)
  • Git (for cloning the repository)

Optional: - Docker (for containerized deployment) - Docker Compose (for orchestrated deployment)

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/mgboyle/App-health-monitor.git
cd App-health-monitor

docs.title: 2. Install Dependencies
docs.text:
pip install -r requirements.txt

Virtual Environment Recommended

It's recommended to use a virtual environment to avoid dependency conflicts:

python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: venv\Scripts\activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 3. Run the Application
docs.text:
python run.py

You should see output similar to:

 * Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000
 * Scheduler started

docs.title: 4. Access the Application
docs.text:
Open your web browser and navigate to:

http://localhost:5000

You should see the App Health Monitor dashboard!

docs.title: Set Environment Variables
docs.text:
Copy the sample configuration file:

cp config.sample.env .env

Edit the .env file to customize your settings:

# Security
SECRET_KEY=change-this-to-a-random-secret-key

# Database
DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///health_monitor.db

# Defaults
DEFAULT_CHECK_INTERVAL=60
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT=30

# Server
PORT=5000

Important: Change the Secret Key

Always change the SECRET_KEY in production to a strong, random value!

docs.title: Using the Web UI
docs.text:
1. Click the "Add Endpoint" button on the dashboard
  2. Fill in the endpoint details:
  3. Name: Example API
  4. URL: https://api.github.com/status
  5. Type: REST
  6. Check Interval: 60 (seconds)
  7. Timeout: 30 (seconds)
  8. Click "Add Endpoint"

docs.title: Using the API
docs.text:
You can also add endpoints programmatically:

curl -X POST http://localhost:5000/endpoints/add \
  -F "name=Example API" \
  -F "url=https://api.github.com/status" \
  -F "endpoint_type=REST" \
  -F "check_interval=60" \
  -F "timeout=30" \
  -F "enabled=on"

docs.title: Web Dashboard
docs.text:
The dashboard automatically refreshes and shows:

  • ✅ Status: Healthy, Failed, or Timeout
  • ⏱️ Response Time: In milliseconds
  • 🕒 Last Checked: Timestamp of the last health check
  • 📊 Statistics: Uptime percentage and success rate

docs.title: API Endpoint
docs.text:
Get health status programmatically:

curl http://localhost:5000/api/health

Response:

{
  "status": "healthy",
  "timestamp": "2024-01-01T12:00:00",
  "endpoints": [
    {
      "name": "Example API",
      "url": "https://api.github.com/status",
      "type": "REST",
      "status": "success",
      "status_code": 200,
      "response_time_ms": 123.45,
      "last_checked": "2024-01-01T12:00:00",
      "error_message": null
    }
  ]
}

docs.text:
Now that you have App Health Monitor running, explore these guides:

  • Installation Guide - Detailed installation options
  • Configuration Guide - All configuration options
  • Managing Endpoints - Learn about endpoint types and features
  • Docker Deployment - Deploy with Docker

docs.title: Docker Quick Start
docs.text:
Prefer to use Docker? Here's the fastest way:

# Using Docker Compose
docker-compose up -d

# Access the application
open http://localhost:5000

That's it! The application is now running in a container with persistent storage.

docs.title: Port Already in Use
docs.text:
If port 5000 is already in use:

PORT=8080 python run.py

docs.title: Database Errors
docs.text:
If you encounter database errors, reset the database:

rm -f instance/health_monitor.db
python run.py

docs.title: Module Not Found
docs.text:
Ensure all dependencies are installed:

pip install -r requirements.txt

For more troubleshooting tips, see the Troubleshooting Guide.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text: This guide helps you solve common issues with App Health Monitor.

docs.title: Python Version Errors
docs.text:
Error: 

Python 3.12 or higher is required

Solution: 

# Check Python version
python --version

# Install Python 3.12
# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt install python3.12

# macOS
brew install python@3.12

# Or use pyenv
pyenv install 3.12.0
pyenv global 3.12.0

docs.title: Dependency Installation Fails
docs.text:
Error: 

ERROR: Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement...

Solutions:

  1. Update pip: 

    pip install --upgrade pip

  2. Clear pip cache: 

    pip cache purge
pip install -r requirements.txt

  3. Use virtual environment: 

    python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Module Not Found
docs.text:
Error: 

ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'flask'

Solution: 

# Ensure virtual environment is activated
source venv/bin/activate

# Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Verify installation
pip list | grep Flask

docs.title: Port Already in Use
docs.text:
Error: 

OSError: [Errno 48] Address already in use

Solutions:

  1. Use different port: 

    PORT=8080 python run.py

  2. Find and kill process using port: 

    # Linux/macOS
lsof -ti:5000 | xargs kill -9

# Or
sudo netstat -tulpn | grep :5000
sudo kill -9 <PID>


# Windows
netstat -ano | findstr :5000
taskkill /PID <PID> /F

docs.title: Database Errors
docs.text:
Error: 

sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError: unable to open database file

Solutions:

  1. Check directory permissions: 

    mkdir -p instance
chmod 755 instance

  2. Reset database: 

    rm -f instance/health_monitor.db
python run.py

  3. Specify database URL: 

    export DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///instance/health_monitor.db
python run.py

docs.title: Secret Key Warning
docs.text:
Error: 

WARNING: Using default SECRET_KEY. Change this in production!

Solution: 

# Generate secure secret key
python -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(32))"

# Set in environment
export SECRET_KEY=<generated-key>

# Or in .env file
echo "SECRET_KEY=<generated-key>" > .env

docs.title: Endpoints Not Being Checked
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Status shows "Not checked yet" - Last checked timestamp never updates - No logs appearing

Causes & Solutions:

  1. Endpoint disabled: 

    Solution: Edit endpoint and enable it

  2. Scheduler not running: 

    # Check application logs
# Should see "Scheduler started"

# Restart application
python run.py

  3. Check interval too long: 

    Solution: Reduce check_interval or manually trigger check

docs.title: All Checks Timing Out
docs.text:
Error: 

Status: timeout
Error: Request timed out after 30 seconds

Solutions:

  1. Increase timeout: 

    Edit endpoint → Timeout: 60 (or higher)

  2. Check network connectivity: 

    # Test endpoint manually
curl -v https://api.example.com/health

# Check DNS resolution
nslookup api.example.com

# Check firewall
telnet api.example.com 443

  3. Check application network access: 

    # From Docker container
docker exec health-monitor curl https://api.example.com/health

docs.title: SSL/TLS Errors
docs.text:
Error: 

SSLError: certificate verify failed

Solutions:

  1. Update certificates: 

    # Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt-get install ca-certificates
sudo update-ca-certificates

# macOS
brew install ca-certificates

  2. Check certificate: 

    openssl s_client -connect api.example.com:443

  3. Temporary workaround (not recommended for production): 

    # Modify health_checker.py
verify=False  # Only for testing!

docs.title: Authentication Failures
docs.text:
Error: 

Status: failure
Status Code: 401
Error: Unauthorized

Solutions:

  1. Verify credentials: 

    # Test manually
curl -u username:password https://api.example.com/health

  2. Check auth type: 

    Ensure correct auth type selected (Basic, Windows, etc.)

  3. Update credentials: 

    Edit endpoint → Update username/password

docs.title: Validation Failures
docs.text:
Error: 

Status: validation_failed
Validation Error: Expected content not found

Solutions:

  1. Check actual response: 

    # View logs to see what was received
# Navigate to endpoint logs

  2. Fix validation rule: 

    Edit endpoint → Adjust validation rules to match actual response

  3. Common issues:

  4. Case sensitivity
  5. JSON path incorrect
  6. Regex pattern too strict
  7. Response format changed

docs.title: Container Won't Start
docs.text:
Error: 

Container exits immediately

Solutions:

  1. Check logs: 

    docker logs health-monitor
docker-compose logs -f

  2. Verify environment variables: 

    docker inspect health-monitor | grep -A 10 Env

  3. Check volume permissions: 

    ls -la ./instance
chmod 755 ./instance

docs.title: Database Not Persisting
docs.text:
Issue: - Data lost after container restart

Solutions:

  1. Check volume mount: 

    # docker-compose.yml
volumes:
  - ./instance:/app/instance

  2. Verify volume: 

    docker-compose down
ls -la ./instance
docker-compose up -d

docs.title: Network Issues in Docker
docs.text:
Issue: - Can't access endpoints from container

Solutions:

  1. Use host network: 

    # docker-compose.yml
network_mode: "host"

  2. Allow egress traffic: 

    # Check Docker network
docker network inspect bridge

  3. Use correct URLs: 

    ❌ http://localhost:8080
✅ http://host.docker.internal:8080 (Docker Desktop)
✅ http://172.17.0.1:8080 (Linux)
✅ http://actual-hostname:8080

docs.title: Slow Dashboard
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Dashboard takes long time to load - UI feels sluggish

Solutions:

  1. Reduce endpoint count: 

    Disable unused endpoints

  2. Increase check intervals: 

    Change from 60s to 300s (5 minutes)

  3. Optimize database: 

    # SQLite
sqlite3 instance/health_monitor.db "VACUUM;"

# Or switch to PostgreSQL

  4. Clear old logs: 

    DELETE FROM health_checks 
WHERE checked_at < datetime('now', '-90 days');

docs.title: High Memory Usage
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Application using excessive RAM - OOM errors

Solutions:

  1. Reduce concurrent checks: 

    Spread out check intervals

  2. Limit log retention: 

    -- Delete logs older than 30 days
DELETE FROM health_checks 
WHERE checked_at < datetime('now', '-30 days');

  3. Increase container memory: 

    # docker-compose.yml
services:
  health-monitor:
    mem_limit: 512m

docs.title: Database Growing Too Large
docs.text:
Issue: - Database file size increasing rapidly

Solutions:

  1. Check database size: 

    du -h instance/health_monitor.db

  2. Archive old data: 

    -- Export to CSV first, then delete
DELETE FROM health_checks 
WHERE checked_at < datetime('now', '-90 days');

VACUUM;

  3. Reduce check frequency: 

    Increase check_interval for non-critical endpoints

docs.title: 404 Not Found
docs.text:
Error: 

404 Not Found

Solutions:

  1. Check URL: 

    # Correct
http://localhost:5000/api/health

# Wrong
http://localhost:5000/health

  2. Verify application is running: 

    curl http://localhost:5000/

  3. Check route registration: 

    # Should show "Running on http://..."
python run.py

docs.title: 500 Internal Server Error
docs.text:
Error: 

500 Internal Server Error

Solutions:

  1. Check application logs: 

    # Look for Python traceback
tail -f /var/log/health-monitor.log

  2. Enable debug mode: 

    FLASK_ENV=development python run.py

  3. Check database connection: 

    from app.app import create_app
app = create_app()
with app.app_context():
    from app.models import db
    db.create_all()

docs.title: CORS Errors
docs.text:
Error: 

Access-Control-Allow-Origin header missing

Solution:

Future Feature

CORS support is planned for a future release.

Workaround: 

# app/app.py
from flask_cors import CORS

def create_app():
    app = Flask(__name__)
    CORS(app)
    # ...

docs.title: Environment Variables Not Working
docs.text:
Issue: - .env file not being loaded

Solutions:

  1. Check file location: 

    # Must be in project root
ls -la .env

  2. Verify format: 

    # Correct
SECRET_KEY=value

# Wrong (no spaces around =)
SECRET_KEY = value

  3. Check for BOM: 

    # Remove BOM if present
dos2unix .env

  4. Load manually: 

    export $(cat .env | xargs)
python run.py

docs.title: Configuration Override Not Working
docs.text:
Issue: - Changes to config don't take effect

Solutions:

  1. Restart application: 

    # Environment variables loaded at startup
python run.py

  2. Check precedence: 

    Environment variables > .env file > config.py defaults

  3. Verify environment: 

    echo $FLASK_ENV
echo $SECRET_KEY

docs.title: "Connection refused"
docs.text:
Cause: Endpoint is not accessible

Solutions: - Verify endpoint is running - Check firewall rules - Verify URL is correct - Check network connectivity

docs.title: "Name or service not known"
docs.text:
Cause: DNS resolution failed

Solutions: - Check domain name spelling - Verify DNS is working - Try IP address instead - Check /etc/hosts or network config

docs.title: "Too many requests"
docs.text:
Cause: Rate limiting on monitored endpoint

Solutions: - Increase check_interval - Contact endpoint provider - Use different credentials - Implement backoff strategy

docs.title: "SSL certificate problem"
docs.text:
Cause: Invalid or expired SSL certificate

Solutions: - Update CA certificates - Contact endpoint provider - Verify certificate validity - Check date/time on server

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
If you can't solve your issue:

  1. Check GitHub Issues:
  2. Search existing issues

  3. Look for similar problems

  4. Enable Debug Logging: 

    LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG python run.py

  5. Gather Information:

  6. Application version
  7. Python version
  8. Operating system
  9. Error messages

  10. Steps to reproduce

  11. Open an Issue:

  12. Visit GitHub Issues
  13. Provide detailed information
  14. Include relevant logs
  15. Describe expected vs actual behavior
